one_line_takeaway: Red-MIRROR shows that coupling a write-once shared memory with dual-phase (local + global) reflection substantially improves LLM agent pentesting on feedback-rich web vulnerabilities (86% vs. 6-50% for baselines on XBOW), but the gains evaporate on blind/low-signal injection and cryptographic challenges where informative feedback is absent -- and its own fine-tuned open-weight model still trails the proprietary backbone by a wide margin even with double the time budget.
key_finding: On XBOW, Red-MIRROR (full) attains 86% success rate (43/50), versus PentestAgent 50% (25/50), AutoPT 46% (23/50), and VulnBot 6% (3/50) -- all under identical DeepSeek-V3.2 backbone and 15-minute budget. On Vulhub, however, Red-MIRROR only TIES PentestAgent at 50% (4/8 each), beating AutoPT (37.5%, 3/8) and VulnBot (0%, 0/8); the paper itself characterizes this as 'comparable to SOTA on Vulhub, clear advantage on XBOW,' not across-the-board dominance. Overall subtask completion rate: Red-MIRROR 93.99% (469/499) vs. PentestAgent 77.77% (388/499), AutoPT 70% (349/499), VulnBot 36.43% (182/499). Ablation (two distinct metrics, kept separate): on the subtask-completion axis, removing both SRMM and Dual-Phase Reflection yields 75% overall subtask completion; SRMM alone raises this to 86% (+11pp); Dual-Phase Reflection alone raises it to 85% (+10pp); the full combination reaches 93.99%, indicating a synergistic (not merely additive) effect. On the separate XBOW success-rate axis (Table 6), the same four configurations score 44% (22/50) -> 64% (32/50, w/o reflection) / 62% (31/50, w/o SRMM) -> 86% (43/50, full), with average TTE on XBOW dropping from 21.77 to 13.19 agent steps for the full configuration (Vulhub TTE stays flat at 7-8 steps across configurations since it lacks input filtering). Token cost scales with capability: $0.10 (neither module) to $0.20 (full) per challenge, versus $0.05 for VulnBot. Fine-tuning result (RQ2) is a modest, not transformative, gain: LoRA-tuned Qwen2.5-14B improves XBOW solved challenges from 2% to 12% (1->6 of 50) and subtask completion from 43.34% to 52.97%, but shows zero improvement on Vulhub (0/8 both before and after), and remains far below DeepSeek-V3.2's 86% XBOW / 93.99% subtask completion -- despite Qwen receiving double the time budget (30 vs 15 minutes).
core_contribution: Red-MIRROR is a multi-agent, LangGraph-orchestrated autonomous web-penetration-testing system that tightly couples three components into a single reasoning backbone: (1) a Shared Recurrent Memory Mechanism (SRMM), a lightweight text-based shared state (inspired by Shared Recurrent Memory Transformer) with a write-once, agent-partitioned, unidirectional (execution agents write, only the Planner reads) memory design intended to prevent context fragmentation and hallucinated self-modification over long attack sessions; (2) a Dual-Phase Reflection mechanism, with Intra-reflection (response-driven per-action retry loop at the Exploiter) and Inter-reflection (Planner-level global replanning using 3x LLM-call majority voting for success detection, flag-prefix checking, and a hard task cap Tmax); and (3) a RAG module grounding payload/attack-pattern generation in curated vulnerability knowledge (SQLi/XSS/SSTI payloads, bypass techniques). The system is instantiated with four agent roles (Planner, Collector, Exploiter, Summarizer/Analyzer). The paper's own contributions beyond the architecture are: a 1,644-pair instruction-tuning dataset (500 NVD CVE descriptions, 239 CAPEC attack patterns, 905 MITRE ATT&CK technique samples) used to LoRA fine-tune Qwen2.5-14B as a self-hosted alternative to the proprietary large model; a new subtask-level benchmark built from 50 selected XBOW challenges plus 8 Critical-severity Vulhub CVEs, enabling fine-grained (per-subtask) rather than only pass/fail evaluation; and an explicit STRIDE-based threat model plus dual-use safeguards discussion. RAG, LLM-based reflection (Reflexion-style), and shared-memory-augmented transformers (SRMT) are cited prior art the paper adapts, not its own invention -- Red-MIRROR's claimed novelty is the tight system-level coupling of these three mechanisms plus the write-once/unidirectional formalization of SRMM (with stated formal properties: monotonic growth, unidirectional flow, bounded context window, deterministic retrieval, aggregation compression).
threat_model: The paper formalizes its assumptions in Section 3.2 using STRIDE. Setting: a black-box, authorized/ethical red-teaming engagement where the target is a web application reachable via HTTP/HTTPS; the simulated adversary (Red-MIRROR itself) uses public tools (e.g., Nmap, SQLMap) without insider privileges or credentials. Dynamic defenses such as input sanitization or WAFs (e.g., ModSecurity) are acknowledged as a source of uncertainty but are explicitly noted as NOT fully exercised in the benchmarks (a stated limitation). STRIDE-mapped mitigations for the agentic system itself (not the target): Tampering and Information Disclosure are countered by SRMM's immutable write-once semantics and gated RAG retrieval; Denial-of-Service is bounded by Dual-Phase Reflection's hard termination cap (max 10 iterations); Elevation-of-Privilege is restricted via a non-destructive execution mode and post-execution verification in the Exploiter Agent; Spoofing and Repudiation are declared explicitly out-of-scope because the system has no identity-level authentication or user-attribution mechanism. The paper frames deployment as requiring user consent and defensive intent, and separately discusses (Section 5.4) dual-use safeguards it recommends for responsible deployment: role-based access control, audit logging/activity auditing, cryptographic watermarking of generated payloads, and RAG knowledge-base gating to trusted/curated sources.
limitations: Blind/low-signal vulnerabilities (time-based or boolean-based blind SQLi/command injection) are the system's dominant failure mode: binary timing signals give low information gain per query, network jitter destabilizes inference, and both Dual-Phase Reflection and SRMM lose their advantage when there is no semantic (content-rich) feedback to act on -- illustrated by a detailed XBEN-029-24 case where the agent stalls during username extraction. No integrated cryptanalysis/symbolic-computation tooling: the system can correctly classify a challenge as cryptographic but cannot execute padding-oracle attacks, weak-RSA key recovery, or similar numeric/algebraic exploitation -- a stated architectural gap, not a reasoning failure. Framework-specific payload placement (e.g., CVE-2022-22963 Spring Cloud Function requiring injection via the spring.cloud.function.routing-expression HTTP header rather than the request body) causes silent exploitation failure despite correct vulnerability identification; the paper attributes some of Red-MIRROR's XBOW losses relative to PentestAgent/AutoPT (e.g., CVE-2021-26084, CVE-2022-22963) to its deliberately restricted, summarized (safety-motivated) PoC handling versus competitors that directly execute crawled PoC scripts. Benchmark scale and composition bias: only 50 XBOW + 8 Vulhub challenges total; blind/low-observability scenarios are only ~8% (4/50) of XBOW, so results may not generalize to low-signal real-world environments; Vulhub covers only known, well-documented Critical CVEs with short exploitation chains, not zero-days. Lab environment lacks real-world defenses: no WAF, IDS, rate limiting, or honeypots were present during evaluation, which the paper explicitly flags as a threat to external validity and notes could substantially change agent behavior. Internal-validity threats acknowledged by the authors: LLM stochasticity (mitigated only partially by low temperature and majority voting), and possible data contamination since XBOW/Vulhub may appear in model pretraining corpora (mitigated by relying on dynamic, non-memorizable flag retrieval). Construct-validity threat: subtask decomposition and completion judgments are manually defined/labeled by the authors, introducing potential subjectivity despite alignment with standard kill-chain phases. The RQ2 fine-tuned-model comparison is confounded by an unequal time budget (Qwen2.5-14B variants got 30 minutes/challenge vs. DeepSeek-V3.2's 15 minutes), which the paper reports but does not control for.

datasets_used: XBOW benchmark: 50 selected challenges (21 Level 1 / 24 Level 2 / 5 Level 3 per prose; Table 2 totals 22/23/5=50), average 9.3 subtasks per challenge (range 3-17), ~8% (4/50) blind/low-observability

Vulhub: 8 real-world Critical-severity CVEs (CVE-2019-10758, CVE-2019-15107, CVE-2021-26084, CVE-2021-42013, CVE-2022-22963, CVE-2022-26134, CVE-2023-22515, CVE-2025-3248), average 4.125 subtasks per CVE (range 2-6)
LoRA fine-tuning dataset (author-constructed, 1,644 instruction-response pairs): 500 CVE descriptions from NVD, 239 CAPEC attack patterns, 905 MITRE ATT&CK technique samples
RAG knowledge base: curated SQLi/XSS payloads, bypass techniques, template/command injection examples for engines such as MySQL, Jinja2, Twig
quantitative_results: XBOW success rate (43/50=86% Red-MIRROR vs. 25/50=50% PentestAgent, 23/50=46% AutoPT, 3/50=6% VulnBot), all on DeepSeek-V3.2, 15-min/challenge
Vulhub success rate (4/8=50% Red-MIRROR, tied with PentestAgent 4/8=50%; AutoPT 3/8=37.5%; VulnBot 0/8=0%)
Overall subtask completion rate (469/499=93.99% Red-MIRROR vs. 388/499=77.77% PentestAgent, 349/499=70% AutoPT, 182/499=36.43% VulnBot)
Ablation, subtask-completion axis: 75% (neither module) -> 86% (SRMM only, +11pp) -> 85% (reflection only, +10pp) -> 93.99% (full)

Ablation, XBOW success-rate axis (Table 6): 44% (22/50) -> 64% (32/50, w/o reflection) -> 62% (31/50, w/o SRMM) -> 86% (43/50, full); avg TTE on XBOW 21.77 -> 13.19 steps for full config; Vulhub TTE stays ~7-8 steps across all configs
Per-challenge token cost: $0.10 (neither module) -> $0.12 (w/o reflection) -> $0.15 (w/o SRMM) -> $0.20 (full), vs. $0.05 for VulnBot

RQ2 fine-tuning: Qwen2.5-14B base->FT XBOW solved 1/50 (2%) -> 6/50 (12%); Vulhub solved 0/8 (0%) -> 0/8 (0%, no change); subtask completion 43.34% (216/499) -> 52.97% (264/499); DeepSeek-V3.2 reference: 86% XBOW solved, 93.99% subtask completion (Qwen variants given 30-min budget vs. DeepSeek's 15-min)
baselines_compared: VulnBot -- the direct architectural foundation Red-MIRROR was built on top of; used as the primary baseline
PentestAgent -- recent SOTA LLM-based pentesting agent, strongest comparator on Vulhub (ties Red-MIRROR)
AutoPT -- recent SOTA LLM-based pentesting agent
Qwen2.5-14B (base, no fine-tuning) and Qwen2.5-14B (LoRA fine-tuned) -- self-hosted mid-scale open-source model variants compared against DeepSeek-V3.2 as the large proprietary backbone
